India Urban Climate Analysis 2020–2025

Brian Kipchumba

2026-05-15

1 Business Understanding

India’s diverse topography means weather patterns vary drastically across cities — from the snow-capped hills of Shimla to the tropical coast of Chennai. Understanding these climate patterns is critical for urban planning, agriculture, disaster preparedness, and environmental policy.

Objectives:

  • Explore climate trends across 98 Indian cities from 2020 to 2025
  • Analyze seasonal patterns in temperature, rainfall, and wind speed
  • Compare climate conditions across geographic regions
  • Identify anomalies and extreme weather events
  • Forecast future temperature trends using time series models

2 Data Understanding

2.1 Load Libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(scales)
library(viridis)
library(corrplot)
library(forecast)
library(tseries)
library(gridExtra)
library(ggthemes)

2.2 Load Dataset

df <- read_csv("Data/popular_cities_weather.csv")
cat("Rows:", nrow(df), "\n")
## Rows: 7056
cat("Columns:", ncol(df), "\n")
## Columns: 9
cat("Cities:", n_distinct(df$city), "\n")
## Cities: 96
cat("Date Range:", as.character(min(df$date)),
    "to", as.character(max(df$date)), "\n")
## Date Range: 2020-01-01 to 2025-12-01

2.3 Preview Data

head(df, 10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 9
##    date                 tavg  tmin  tmax  prcp wspd   pres  tsun city  
##    <dttm>              <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <lgl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> 
##  1 2020-01-01 00:00:00  24.9  19.2  29.9    0  NA    1013. 13800 Mumbai
##  2 2020-02-01 00:00:00  27.3  21    32.6    0  NA    1013. 11220 Mumbai
##  3 2020-03-01 00:00:00  27.7  22.6  31.9    0  NA    1011. 12900 Mumbai
##  4 2020-04-01 00:00:00  30.2  26.4  33.9    0  NA    1010. 14220 Mumbai
##  5 2020-05-01 00:00:00  31.1  28.6  34      0  NA    1007. 15960 Mumbai
##  6 2020-06-01 00:00:00  29.2  26    32.3  525  NA    1004.  8340 Mumbai
##  7 2020-07-01 00:00:00  28.1  25.4  30.5 1502. NA    1003.  4620 Mumbai
##  8 2020-08-01 00:00:00  27.6  25    29.6  826. NA    1003.  2760 Mumbai

## 10 2020-10-01 00:00:00  29.2  25.6  32.7  224  NA    1007.  7920 Mumbai

2.4 Data Structure

glimpse(df)
## Rows: 7,056
## Columns: 9
## $ date <dttm> 2020-01-01, 2020-02-01, 2020-03-01, 2020-04-01, 2020-05-01, 2020…

## $ city <chr> "Mumbai", "Mumbai", "Mumbai", "Mumbai", "Mumbai", "Mumbai", "Mumb…

2.5 Summary Statistics

summary(df)
##       date                          tavg           tmin            tmax

2.6 2.6 Missing Values

missing_vals <- df %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = "Column",
               values_to = "Missing") %>%
  mutate(Percentage = round(Missing / nrow(df) * 100, 2))

print(missing_vals)
## # A tibble: 9 × 3
##   Column Missing Percentage
##   <chr>    <int>      <dbl>
## 1 date         0       0   
## 2 tavg       539       7.64
## 3 tmin       604       8.56
## 4 tmax       625       8.86
## 5 prcp       930      13.2 
## 6 wspd      7056     100   
## 7 pres       622       8.82
## 8 tsun      4651      65.9 
## 9 city         0       0
ggplot(missing_vals, aes(x = reorder(Column, -Percentage), y = Percentage)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#EF5350") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Percentage, "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "Missing Values by Column (%)",
       x = "Column", y = "Missing (%)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

3 Data Preparation

3.1 Fix Date & Add Features

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    date      = as.Date(date),
    year      = year(date),
    month     = month(date, label = TRUE),
    month_num = month(date),
    season    = case_when(
      month_num %in% c(12, 1, 2)  ~ "Winter",
      month_num %in% c(3, 4, 5)   ~ "Summer",
      month_num %in% c(6, 7, 8)   ~ "Monsoon",
      month_num %in% c(9, 10, 11) ~ "Post-Monsoon"
    )
  )

cat("Season distribution:\n")
## Season distribution:
print(table(df$season))
## 
##      Monsoon Post-Monsoon       Summer       Winter 
##         1764         1764         1764         1764
cat("\nNew shape:", nrow(df), "x", ncol(df), "\n")
## 
## New shape: 7056 x 13

3.2 Handle Missing Values

# Drop wspd column (100% missing)
df <- df %>% select(-wspd)

# Fill remaining missing values with city mean
df <- df %>%
  group_by(city) %>%
  mutate(
    tavg = ifelse(is.na(tavg), mean(tavg, na.rm = TRUE), tavg),
    tmin = ifelse(is.na(tmin), mean(tmin, na.rm = TRUE), tmin),
    tmax = ifelse(is.na(tmax), mean(tmax, na.rm = TRUE), tmax),
    prcp = ifelse(is.na(prcp), 0, prcp),
    pres = ifelse(is.na(pres), mean(pres, na.rm = TRUE), pres),
    tsun = ifelse(is.na(tsun), mean(tsun, na.rm = TRUE), tsun)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

cat("Missing values after cleaning:\n")
## Missing values after cleaning:
print(colSums(is.na(df)))
##      date      tavg      tmin      tmax      prcp      pres      tsun      city 
##         0         0         0         0         0         0      4464         0 
##      year     month month_num    season 
##         0         0         0         0
cat("\nFinal shape:", nrow(df), "x", ncol(df), "\n")
## 
## Final shape: 7056 x 12
cat("Data preparation complete!\n")
## Data preparation complete!

3.3 Fix Remaining tsun Missing Values

# Replace remaining tsun NAs with overall mean
df <- df %>%
  mutate(tsun = ifelse(is.na(tsun), mean(tsun, na.rm = TRUE), tsun))

cat("Missing values after final cleaning:\n")
## Missing values after final cleaning:
print(colSums(is.na(df)))
##      date      tavg      tmin      tmax      prcp      pres      tsun      city 
##         0         0         0         0         0         0         0         0 
##      year     month month_num    season 
##         0         0         0         0
cat("\nFinal shape:", nrow(df), "x", ncol(df), "\n")
## 
## Final shape: 7056 x 12

4 Temperature Analysis

4.1 National Average Temperature Trend

temp_trend <- df %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarise(avg_temp = mean(tavg, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(temp_trend, aes(x = date, y = avg_temp)) +
  geom_line(color = "#EF5350", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, 
              color = "#1565C0", fill = "#BBDEFB") +
  labs(title = "National Average Temperature Trend (2020–2025)",
       x = "Date", y = "Average Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

The chart shows clear seasonal temperature cycles across India from 2020 to 2025. Temperatures peak during Summer (March–May) reaching up to 32°C and drop during Winter (December–February) to around 20°C. The smooth blue trend line shows a slight overall warming pattern over the 5-year period.

4.2 Monthly Temperature Distribution

ggplot(df, aes(x = month, y = tavg, fill = month)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE, outlier.alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "C") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Temperature Distribution Across All Cities",
       x = "Month", y = "Average Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal()

Temperatures rise steadily from January peaking in May–July around 30°C across all cities. December and January show the widest spread, reflecting the contrast between cold northern cities like Shimla and warm southern cities like Chennai during winter.

4.3 Top 10 Hottest & Coldest Cities

city_temp <- df %>%
  group_by(city) %>%
  summarise(avg_temp = mean(tavg, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

top_hot  <- city_temp %>% slice_max(avg_temp, n = 10)
top_cold <- city_temp %>% slice_min(avg_temp, n = 10)

p1 <- ggplot(top_hot, aes(x = reorder(city, avg_temp), y = avg_temp)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#EF5350") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_temp, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Hottest Cities", x = "", y = "Avg Temp (°C)") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(top_cold, aes(x = reorder(city, -avg_temp), y = avg_temp)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#42A5F5") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_temp, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Coldest Cities", x = "", y = "Avg Temp (°C)") +
  theme_minimal()

grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)

Hottest cities are concentrated in South India — coastal and inland cities like Tiruchirappalli and Kozhikode maintain high temperatures year-round due to their tropical location. Coldest cities are in North India — Srinagar and Shimla are hill stations with significantly lower average temperatures due to high altitude.

4.4 Seasonal Temperature Analysis

df %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(
    Average = mean(tavg, na.rm = TRUE),
    Minimum = mean(tmin, na.rm = TRUE),
    Maximum = mean(tmax, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Average, Minimum, Maximum),
               names_to = "type", values_to = "temp") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = season, y = temp, fill = type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(temp, 1)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#42A5F5", "#66BB6A", "#EF5350")) +
  labs(title = "Average Temperature by Season",
       x = "Season", y = "Temperature (°C)", fill = "Type") +
  theme_minimal()

Summer records the highest maximum temperatures (35.2°C) while Winter has the lowest minimum (14.9°C). Monsoon maintains warm average temperatures despite heavy rainfall. The temperature range is widest in Winter reflecting the contrast between northern and southern cities. —

5 Rainfall Analysis

5.1 Monthly Rainfall Pattern

df %>%
  group_by(month, month_num) %>%
  summarise(avg_rain = mean(prcp, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(month_num) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = avg_rain, fill = avg_rain)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B") +
  labs(title = "Average Monthly Rainfall Across All Cities",
       x = "Month", y = "Average Rainfall (mm)", fill = "Rain (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()

Rainfall is heavily concentrated in the Monsoon months (June–September) with July recording the highest average of ~320mm across all cities. The dry season from January to April shows near-zero rainfall, confirming India’s strong monsoon-driven precipitation pattern.

5.2 Top 10 Wettest Cities

df %>%
  group_by(city) %>%
  summarise(total_rain = sum(prcp, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(total_rain, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(city, total_rain), y = total_rain)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#1565C0") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(total_rain, 0)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Wettest Cities (Total Rainfall 2020–2025)",
       x = "", y = "Total Rainfall (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()

Mangalore is the wettest city with over 25,000mm total rainfall over 5 years, followed by Kozhikode and several Mumbai metropolitan cities. All top 10 wettest cities are located on India’s western coastal belt, which receives the heaviest Southwest Monsoon rainfall.

5.3 Rainfall by Season

df %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(avg_rain = mean(prcp, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(season, -avg_rain), y = avg_rain, fill = season)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_rain, 1)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#1565C0", "#EF5350", "#66BB6A", "#FFA726")) +
  labs(title = "Average Rainfall by Season",
       x = "Season", y = "Average Rainfall (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()

Monsoon season dominates with 239.7mm average rainfall — more than double Post-Monsoon. Winter is the driest season with only 17.4mm, confirming that over 58% of India’s annual rainfall comes during the June–September Monsoon period.

6 Pressure & Correlation Analysis

6.1 Average Pressure by Month

df %>%
  group_by(month, month_num) %>%
  summarise(avg_pres = mean(pres, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(month_num) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = avg_pres, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(color = "#AB47BC", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#AB47BC", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Average Atmospheric Pressure by Month",
       x = "Month", y = "Pressure (hPa)") +
  theme_minimal()

Atmospheric pressure follows an inverse pattern to rainfall — pressure drops sharply from January to June/July coinciding exactly with the arrival of the Monsoon. The lowest pressure in June–July (1002 hPa) corresponds to the peak rainfall months, confirming that low pressure systems drive India’s Monsoon precipitation.

6.2 6.2 Correlation Matrix

cor_data <- df %>%
  select(tavg, tmin, tmax, prcp, pres, tsun) %>%
  drop_na()

cor_matrix <- cor(cor_data)

corrplot(cor_matrix,
         method      = "color",
         type        = "upper",
         addCoef.col = "black",
         tl.col      = "black",
         tl.srt      = 45,
         title       = "Correlation Matrix of Climate Variables",
         mar         = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

Temperature variables are strongly correlated with each other (0.94–0.95). The strongest negative correlation is between minimum temperature and pressure (-0.83) — as temperatures rise, pressure drops driving Monsoon rainfall. Rainfall and sunshine show a negative correlation (-0.39) as expected — rainy months have significantly less sunshine.

7 City Comparison

7.1 Temperature Trends — Major Cities

major_cities <- c("Mumbai", "Delhi", "Chennai", 
                  "Kolkata", "Bangalore", "Hyderabad")

df %>%
  filter(city %in% major_cities) %>%
  group_by(city, date) %>%
  summarise(avg_temp = mean(tavg, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = avg_temp, color = city)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Temperature Trends — Major Indian Cities (2020–2025)",
       x = "Date", y = "Average Temperature (°C)", color = "City") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Delhi shows the most extreme seasonal variation — dropping to 14°C in winter and rising to 34°C in summer. Mumbai and Chennai maintain stable temperatures year-round due to their coastal location. Bangalore stays the coolest among southern cities due to its high elevation.

8 Stationarity Test — Mumbai

# Extract Mumbai temperature time series
mumbai_ts <- df %>%
  filter(city == "Mumbai") %>%
  arrange(date) %>%
  pull(tavg)

mumbai_ts <- ts(mumbai_ts, start = c(2020, 1), frequency = 12)

# Plot the time series first
autoplot(mumbai_ts) +
  labs(title = "Mumbai Temperature Time Series",
       x = "Year", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal()

Clear seasonal cycles every year No upward/downward drift — confirms stationarity

8.1 ADF Test (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)

# ADF Test — tests for stationarity
# H0: Series is NON-stationary
# H1: Series IS stationary
adf_result <- adf.test(mumbai_ts)
print(adf_result)
## 
##  Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
## 
## data:  mumbai_ts
## Dickey-Fuller = -3.7855, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.02433
## alternative hypothesis: stationary
cat("\n")
if (adf_result$p.value < 0.05) {
  cat("✅ p-value =", round(adf_result$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("✅ Series is STATIONARY — no differencing needed\n")
} else {
  cat("❌ p-value =", round(adf_result$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("❌ Series is NON-STATIONARY — differencing needed\n")
}
## ✅ p-value = 0.0243 
## ✅ Series is STATIONARY — no differencing needed

p-value = 0.0243 (less than 0.05) Series is STATIONARY — no differencing needed

8.2 ACF & PACF Plots

# ACF and PACF help determine ARIMA parameters
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(mumbai_ts,  main = "ACF — Mumbai Temperature")
pacf(mumbai_ts, main = "PACF — Mumbai Temperature")

ACF shows significant spikes at lag 1.0 — strong seasonality PACF cuts off quickly — suggests AR terms needed

8.3 ARIMA Model — Mumbai

# auto.arima automatically selects best ARIMA parameters
fit_mumbai <- auto.arima(mumbai_ts, 
                          seasonal = TRUE,
                          stepwise = FALSE,
                          approximation = FALSE)

cat("Best ARIMA Model for Mumbai:\n")
## Best ARIMA Model for Mumbai:
summary(fit_mumbai)
## Series: mumbai_ts 
## ARIMA(0,0,0)(2,1,0)[12] 
## 
## Coefficients:
##          sar1     sar2
##       -0.6805  -0.4717
## s.e.   0.1267   0.1293
## 
## sigma^2 = 0.6089:  log likelihood = -73.69
## AIC=153.38   AICc=153.81   BIC=159.67
## 
## Training set error measures:
##                      ME      RMSE       MAE        MPE     MAPE      MASE
## Training set 0.03772381 0.7003611 0.5155291 0.09907398 1.838487 0.6444113
##                   ACF1
## Training set 0.1204975

8.4 Forecast — Next 12 Months

forecast_mumbai <- forecast(fit_mumbai, h = 12)

autoplot(forecast_mumbai) +
  labs(title = "Mumbai Temperature Forecast — Next 12 Months (2026)",
       x = "Year", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(fontface = "bold"))

8.5 Model Accuracy

acc <- accuracy(fit_mumbai)
cat("Model Accuracy Metrics:\n")
## Model Accuracy Metrics:
print(acc)
##                      ME      RMSE       MAE        MPE     MAPE      MASE
## Training set 0.03772381 0.7003611 0.5155291 0.09907398 1.838487 0.6444113
##                   ACF1
## Training set 0.1204975
cat("\nForecast Values for Next 12 Months:\n")
## 
## Forecast Values for Next 12 Months:
print(round(forecast_mumbai$mean, 2))
##        Jan   Feb   Mar   Apr   May   Jun   Jul   Aug   Sep   Oct   Nov   Dec
## 2026 25.37 27.86 28.90 30.21 30.45 29.68 27.83 28.13 27.71 29.66 28.68 27.26

The ARIMA(0,0,0)(2,1,0)[12] model captures Mumbai’s seasonal temperature pattern with high accuracy — MAPE of only 1.84% meaning predictions are within 2% of actual values. The 2026 forecast shows peak temperatures of 30.5°C in May dropping to 25.4°C in January, consistent with Mumbai’s historical seasonal pattern.

9 Stationarity Test — Delhi

delhi_ts <- df %>%
  filter(city == "Delhi") %>%
  arrange(date) %>%
  pull(tavg)

delhi_ts <- ts(delhi_ts, start = c(2020, 1), frequency = 12)

# Plot Delhi time series
autoplot(delhi_ts) +
  labs(title = "Delhi Temperature Time Series",
       x = "Year", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal()

9.1 Delhi ADF Test

adf_delhi <- adf.test(delhi_ts)
print(adf_delhi)
## 
##  Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
## 
## data:  delhi_ts
## Dickey-Fuller = -6.4115, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.01
## alternative hypothesis: stationary
cat("\n")
if (adf_delhi$p.value < 0.05) {
  cat("✅ p-value =", round(adf_delhi$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("✅ Delhi series is STATIONARY — no differencing needed\n")
} else {
  cat("❌ p-value =", round(adf_delhi$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("❌ Delhi series is NON-STATIONARY — differencing needed\n")
}
## ✅ p-value = 0.01 
## ✅ Delhi series is STATIONARY — no differencing needed

9.2 8.8 Delhi ACF & PACF

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(delhi_ts,  main = "ACF — Delhi Temperature")
pacf(delhi_ts, main = "PACF — Delhi Temperature")

9.3 ARIMA Model — Delhi

fit_delhi <- auto.arima(delhi_ts,
                         seasonal = TRUE,
                         stepwise = FALSE,
                         approximation = FALSE)

cat("Best ARIMA Model for Delhi:\n")
## Best ARIMA Model for Delhi:
summary(fit_delhi)
## Series: delhi_ts 
## ARIMA(0,0,1)(0,1,2)[12] 
## 
## Coefficients:
##          ma1     sma1    sma2
##       0.6761  -0.9693  0.3538
## s.e.  0.1097   0.1849  0.2191
## 
## sigma^2 = 0.977:  log likelihood = -89.11
## AIC=186.21   AICc=186.94   BIC=194.59
## 
## Training set error measures:
##                      ME      RMSE       MAE       MPE    MAPE      MASE
## Training set 0.07908028 0.8794658 0.6484723 0.2709128 2.68525 0.4588247
##                     ACF1
## Training set -0.03974058

9.4 Delhi Forecast — Next 12 Months

forecast_delhi <- forecast(fit_delhi, h = 12)

autoplot(forecast_delhi) +
  labs(title = "Delhi Temperature Forecast — Next 12 Months (2026)",
       x = "Year", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(fontface = "bold"))

The Delhi ARIMA(0,0,1)(0,1,2)[12] model forecasts a peak temperature of 33°C in June 2026 dropping sharply to 16°C in December 2026. The narrow confidence interval (blue shading) indicates high forecast confidence. Delhi’s extreme seasonal swing of nearly 20°C between winter and summer is clearly captured by the model.

9.5 Mumbai vs Delhi Forecast Comparison

mumbai_fc <- as.numeric(forecast_mumbai$mean)
delhi_fc  <- as.numeric(forecast_delhi$mean)
months    <- c("Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
               "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec")

forecast_compare <- data.frame(
  Month  = factor(months, levels = months),
  Mumbai = mumbai_fc,
  Delhi  = delhi_fc
) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Mumbai, Delhi),
               names_to  = "City",
               values_to = "Temperature")

ggplot(forecast_compare, aes(x = Month, y = Temperature,
                              color = City, group = City)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#EF5350", "#1565C0")) +
  labs(title = "2026 Temperature Forecast — Mumbai vs Delhi",
       x = "Month", y = "Forecasted Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(fontface = "bold"))

The comparison chart highlights the fundamental climate difference between the two cities. Delhi (red) shows extreme seasonal variation — starting at 13°C in January, peaking at 33°C in June, then dropping back to 16°C in December. Mumbai (blue) remains remarkably stable throughout the year, staying between 25°C and 30°C — never too cold, never extreme. This reflects the difference between Delhi’s continental inland climate and Mumbai’s tropical coastal climate moderated by the Arabian Sea.

10 Conclusion & Recommendations

10.1 Key Findings

This project analyzed monthly climate data for 96 Indian cities from 2020 to 2025, covering temperature, rainfall, pressure, and sunshine duration.

Temperature:

  • India shows strong seasonal temperature cycles with peak temperatures in May–June (Summer) averaging 28.6°C and lowest in December–January (Winter) averaging 20.6°C
  • Tiruchirappalli and Kozhikode are the hottest cities (~29°C average)
  • Srinagar and Shimla are the coldest cities (14–16°C average)
  • Delhi shows the most extreme seasonal variation — 21°C range between winter and summer

Rainfall:

  • Monsoon season (June–September) dominates rainfall with 239.7mm average — more than double all other seasons combined
  • Mangalore is the wettest city with over 25,000mm total rainfall over 5 years
  • All top 10 wettest cities are located on India’s western coastal belt

Pressure & Correlation:

  • Atmospheric pressure has a strong inverse relationship with temperature (−0.79) and rainfall (−0.41)
  • Pressure drops to its lowest in June–July coinciding exactly with peak Monsoon rainfall
  • Rainfall and sunshine are negatively correlated (−0.39) — rainy months have significantly less sunshine

Forecasting:

  • Both Mumbai and Delhi temperature series are stationary — confirmed by ADF test (p < 0.05)
  • Mumbai ARIMA(0,0,0)(2,1,0)[12] — MAPE of 1.84% — highly accurate
  • Delhi ARIMA(0,0,1)(0,1,2)[12] — MAPE of 2.69% — highly accurate
  • 2026 forecasts show temperatures will follow historical seasonal patterns

10.2 Recommendations

  • Urban Planning: Cities with high summer temperatures like Tiruchirappalli should invest in green cover, water bodies, and heat-resilient infrastructure
  • Agriculture: Farmers in rain-dependent regions should use these forecasts for crop planning especially during irregular Monsoon years
  • Disaster Preparedness: Coastal cities like Mumbai, Mangalore, and Kozhikode should strengthen flood management systems given consistently high rainfall
  • Climate Monitoring: The slight warming trend observed in the national average temperature warrants closer environmental monitoring and policy intervention

Analysis completed using R 4.5.0 | Data: Indian Cities Weather Dataset 2020–2025
